But it's not just the common cause of self-righteousness, there is also a thread of the underdogs fighting back against the "big guys" using the tools of the internet. And the inherent lulz of that.

It is easy to rally an anonymous community around a common enemy

Scientology: "They are a cult attacking free speech through litigation!"
GamerGate: "Video games reviews are pay-to-play"
GameStop: "Greedy hedgefunds are ripping apart beloved retail outlets"

A lot of current events can be tied back to GamerGate, but imo, for things like Gamestop (or #OpGamestop, as it would have been called) can be tied to the lulz-oriented but effective mass organizing that first materialized IRL with anti-scientology https://t.co/SBX7QtRqF1

RT @ejfoxverbose: Stopping by GitHub HQ 3.0 (at January 27, 2014 at 09:55AM )

@wherestherevolt We don’t have to have consensus on tactics, only a shared goal. Where we diverge in tactics we separate logistically but not in partnership. An anarchist framework is helpful because you can hold many variant subviews but the root of the tree is a shared core freedom.

@wherestherevolt For me, each decision faced:
- Have we listened to the weakest among us
- Have we listened to the ones who have cared enough to do the research 
- Are we valuing human life and enjoyment over profit and colonization 

You just start there and end up with very different answers

@wherestherevolt I saw a speaker recently, I wish I could remember her name. She said we dont need to answer each of these questions now. What we need is a framework for answering them when they come up in a way that bends towards our shared goals and beliefs most reliably.
